description: Mapping of FDA 21 CFR 820 (Quality System Regulation / QMSR) requirements to NIST 800-53 Rev 5 controls

source.disclaimer: IMPORTANT: These mappings are interpretive guidance created to assist with compliance research. They are NOT official FDA or NIST crosswalks. For authoritative mappings, consult: (1) FDA guidance on Quality System Regulation, (2) NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5, (3) AAMI TIR57:2016 for medical device security risk management, (4) Your organization's quality and compliance counsel. These mappings should be validated against your specific implementation context.
references: NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 - Security and Privacy Controls
AAMI TIR57:2016 - Principles for medical device security — Risk management
source.methodology: Mappings based on control objective alignment between QSR requirements and NIST 800-53 control families. Coverage indicates degree of overlap: 'full' = direct alignment, 'partial' = related but not complete, 'related' = conceptually connected.

mappings.rationale: Design controls (820.30) require a structured development lifecycle with planning, inputs, outputs, reviews, verification, and validation — directly aligned with SA-3 SDLC requirements
Design input requirements (820.30(c)) must include safety and performance requirements; for cyber devices this encompasses security engineering principles during design
Design verification (820.30(e)) requires confirmation that design output meets design input requirements through testing and evaluation
Design validation (820.30(f)) under actual or simulated use conditions may include static and dynamic analysis for software-containing devices
Software validation (820.70(i)) requires validation of computer software for its intended use according to an established protocol — directly maps to developer testing requirements
Nonconforming product procedures (820.90) address identification and disposition of products not meeting specifications, including cybersecurity defects in software
CAPA (820.100) requires systematic investigation, corrective action planning, verification, and tracking — directly aligned with POA&M for security findings
CAPA is the primary mechanism for tracking and remediating cybersecurity vulnerabilities in the quality system, mapping directly to flaw remediation
Purchasing controls (820.50) require evaluation of suppliers and purchased components — for cyber devices this includes third-party software and supply chain security
Records requirements (820.180) mandate retention for the design and expected life of the device (minimum 2 years), aligning with audit record retention requirements
Complaint files (820.198) require evaluation of complaints including potential MDR-reportable events, relating to incident reporting for cybersecurity issues affecting device safety
